set.title: Sample: Safe async data loading
set.description: A built-in sample lesson so you can try MergeLearn before authoring your own.
set.objective: Load remote data safely: await in order, handle failure, and avoid a common race.

back.explanationMarkdown:
An `async` function ALWAYS returns a Promise, even when its body returns a plain value. `f()` is therefore a `Promise`, and `typeof` a promise is `"object"`. To read the resolved value you must `await f()` or call `.then(...)`.

The mental model: `async` wraps the return value in `Promise.resolve(...)`.

options.feedback: No. The value 1 is wrapped in a Promise before it leaves the function.
Yes. An async function returns a Promise, and typeof a Promise is "object".
No. The function does return something: a Promise.

front.prompt: Why can awaiting requests in a loop one-by-one be slower than awaiting them together, and when is the slow version still correct?

back.shortAnswer: Serial awaits wait for each request before starting the next; Promise.all runs them concurrently. Serial is right when each call depends on the previous result.
back.explanationMarkdown:
`for (const x of xs) { await req(x); }` starts request N+1 only after N resolves, so total time is the SUM of latencies. `await Promise.all(xs.map(req))` starts them all at once, so total time is the MAX. Prefer `Promise.all` for independent calls.

Keep the serial form only when each step needs the previous step's result (a true data dependency), or when you must limit concurrency.

interaction.placeholder: Explain the time difference, then the exception.

front.prompt: Order the lines of an async function that loads a user and returns its name safely.

back.shortAnswer: Signature, await the request, guard the response, await JSON, return the field, close.
back.explanationMarkdown: Each line depends on the one above it. You cannot check `res.ok` before `await fetch(...)` gives you `res`, and you cannot read `user.name` before `await res.json()` produces `user`. The `res.ok` guard must sit BEFORE parsing, or a failed request parses an error body. The order is forced by data and control flow, not style.

front.prompt: What is the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled?

back.shortAnswer: Promise.all rejects as soon as any input rejects; Promise.allSettled always resolves with one result object per input.
back.explanationMarkdown:
`Promise.all` is fail-fast: the first rejection rejects the whole thing and the other results are lost. `Promise.allSettled` waits for every promise and resolves to an array of `{ status: "fulfilled", value }` or `{ status: "rejected", reason }`.

Use `all` when any failure should abort the batch; use `allSettled` when you want every outcome, for example rendering a dashboard where one failed widget should not blank the others.
